title: Spreading manure and timing the spread
subtitle: When and how to apply farmyard manure to pasture and vegetable ground.
excerpt: Farmyard manure from a small holding is a valuable soil amendment, but the timing of application determines whether it builds fertility or causes nutrient run-off and legal risk. This covers the spreading restrictions, the best seasonal windows, and the practical approach for small quantities spread by hand.
sourceNotes: DEFRA Nitrate Vulnerable Zone guidance for England (2020). Farming and Wildlife Advisory Group (FWAG), manure management for small farms. AHDB nutrient management guide (RB209).

content.text: Farmyard manure (FYM) from stock bedding is ready to spread once the straw has composted. The heap should have heated and then cooled over six to eight weeks. Fresh manure spread directly on pasture damages the grass and creates a strong odour. Well-composted manure breaks down faster on the soil. It also reduces the loss of nitrogen as ammonia.
Do not spread within 10 m of a watercourse. If your holding falls within a
, there are closed periods in which spreading solid FYM is not permitted (typically 1 October to 31 January on arable land; check current guidance for grass).
has additional restrictions and longer closed periods. Do not spread on waterlogged, frozen, or steeply sloping ground where run-off risk is high.

The best spreading windows for pasture are late winter (February) when the soil is not waterlogged and grass will grow through the manure quickly in spring, and late summer to early autumn (August to September) after the main grazing season. Spread in both windows if the heap is large. Avoid spreading just before heavy rain is forecast, which risks washing nutrients to a watercourse.

For a small holding without a tractor-mounted spreader, use a muck fork and wheelbarrow. Load the barrow from the heap, move to the field, and fork the manure out in a thin, even layer rather than dumping in piles. A layer 5 to 7 cm deep incorporates well without smothering grass. Leave the spread field for at least six weeks before grazing to allow the manure to incorporate and reduce parasite survival on the herbage.
